oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Wound healing and management in cattle
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Miesner, Matt
oai_dc:dc.dc:subject: cattle
wound
management
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: Cattle are susceptible to injury from natural environmental hazards, handling practices, as well as their inherent curiosity, flighty nature, and herd mentality. Cattle may not be observed closely from day to day; thus, self-inflicted wound management is more often focused on second intention or delayed primary healing practices when presented. However, when creating surgically induced wounds, we should always consider healing principles during pre-, intra-, and post-operative management to properly prevent or possibly manage inevitable variations in healing between patients. During this presentation, we will dis­cuss some risk factors and the nature of some environmental and surgical wounds that are presented in cattle practice and how they are managed. We will apply wound healing principles during management as well as discussions of anesthetic, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial use in context of food animal practice. Some discussion of over-the-counter products will also be presented.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: American Association of Bovine Practitioners Conference Proceedings; No. 1 (2026): 2026 Recent Graduate Conference Proceedings; 73-74
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 American Association of Bovine Practitioners
